coverage: representative slice of the shipped 229-quality vocabulary: 48 qualities (24 curated + 24 extra), 2 samples each
improvement_over_previous: After refining the impact-families (split composure -> calm/resilient; reflective qualities -> clarity, learning -> craft; pruned ill-fitting impacts), the LLM judge's appropriateness rose 0.78 -> 0.89 and overall 0.83 -> 0.88. Specific fixes confirmed: 'charity -> saved time' is gone (now 'shared the load'/'made someone's day'); 'courage -> turned a hard week around' now reads as apt.
verdict: The refactor largely worked: the prior charity/courage mismatches are fixed, lifting appropriateness from 0.78 to 0.89. A few weak pairings persist (introspection still lands on 'saved us a lot of time' via the clarity family; artistry -> 'made things clearer'), and variety is capped by a small clause pool plus a few identical sample pairs.
weaknesses: introspection -> 'saved us a lot of time' (clarity family) is still a poor fit; reflection is not a time-saver.
artistry -> 'made things clearer' (creativity family) is loose; artistry is expressive, not clarifying.
a few qualities still produce byte-identical sample pairs (low within-quality variety).
raw model output is all-lowercase (the web app prettifies for display).
note: LLM-as-judge qualitative benchmark on the actual shipped model, recorded honestly alongside the deterministic Python metrics. The cheap deterministic appropriateness still reads ~100%; the judge's stricter 0.89 is the more honest figure. Not a capability-class claim.
